metadata.working_title: Cross-species alignment along the chronological axis reveals evolutionary effects on structural development of the human brain

abstract_sketch.problem: Comparative neuroscience typically aligns human and nonhuman primate brains spatially, but cannot directly quantify disproportionate anatomical development of the human brain along the temporal axis.
abstract_sketch.gap: It remains unknown whether brain developmental phases of human and macaque can be conjugated through a shared chronological model of brain anatomy, and what role anatomy plays in that conjugation.
abstract_sketch.approach: We built brain structure based cross-species age prediction models from gray matter volume and four DTI-derived microstructural indices (FA, MD, AD, RD) in 370 humans (8-14 years) and 181 rhesus macaques (2-4 years), applied them within- and across-species, and defined a brain cross-species age gap (BCAP) index as a percentile-rank difference.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Within-species models predicted age in macaques (R = 0.5729, P < 0.001, MAE = 0.3758) and humans (R = 0.6153, P < 0.001, MAE = 1.1236). Cross-species prediction was asymmetric: the macaque-trained model predicted human age (R = 0.4823, P < 0.001, MAE = 8.3610) better than the human-trained model predicted macaque age (R = 0.2898, P < 0.001, MAE = 7.6157). The human brain age gap correlated positively with actual age (R = 0.9813, P < 0.001, MAE = 2.7120), the macaque gap correlated negatively (R = -0.3759, P < 0.001, MAE = 4.8697). BCAP was associated with picture vocabulary (R = 0.1588, P = 0.0323) and visual sensitivity (R = -0.2051, P = 0.0056) performance in humans, and highlighted arcuate fasciculus and prefrontal cortex as the strongest evolution-associated features.
abstract_sketch.impact: Embedding cross-species brain anatomy along a shared chronological axis provides a stable, interpretable index (BCAP) for quantifying disproportionate anatomical development, linking language pathways and higher-order cognitive areas to human-specific developmental divergence.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Understanding evolutionary effects on the human brain is a fundamental goal of comparative neuroscience; rhesus macaques are a key surrogate due to high homology with humans.
Spatially aligned comparative neuroimaging has revealed cortical expansion, hemispherical asymmetry, and differences in arcuate fasciculus myelination and connectivity between humans and macaques.

Brain anatomy develops along a temporal axis in both species (sensory before association cortex in humans; pubertal cortical surface area expansion in macaques), but spatial alignment alone may not resolve chronological developmental divergence.

We propose to embed human and macaque brain anatomy on a shared chronological axis via brain structure based cross-species age prediction models that use GMV and white-matter microstructure (FA, MD, AD, RD) as features.

We introduce the brain cross-species age gap (BCAP) as a percentile-rank index that quantifies cross-species developmental divergence per subject and link it to brain areas, white-matter tracts, and behavioral phenotypes.

subsections.title: Intra- and inter-/cross-species age prediction
content_bullets: Context: four prediction models were built (human->human, macaque->macaque, human->macaque, macaque->human) with 62 macaque features and 225 human features present across all 100 iterations.
Approach: 10-fold cross-validation with Pearson R and MAE as evaluation metrics.

Finding: intra-species models predicted well (macaque R = 0.5729, P < 0.001, MAE = 0.3758; human R = 0.6153, P < 0.001, MAE = 1.1236). Cross-species: macaque->human R = 0.4823, MAE = 8.3610; human->macaque R = 0.2898, MAE = 7.6157.

Stability: with 117/239 features (macaque R = 0.5825, MAE = 0.3675; human R = 0.6039, MAE = 1.1388) and with top-62 shared features (macaque R = 0.5729, MAE = 0.3760; human R = 0.6818, MAE = 1.0025), the macaque->human asymmetry persisted.
Interpretation: the trained macaque model consistently outperforms the trained human model when predicting the other species' chronological age, indicating an intrinsic cross-species imbalance.

subsections.title: Age effects on |Delta brain age| for cross-species prediction
content_bullets: Context: prediction errors may grow or shrink with actual age, revealing where cross-species models diverge.
Approach: Pearson's correlation between |Delta brain age| and actual age in each species.

Finding: human |Delta brain age| positively correlated with actual age (R = 0.9813, P < 0.001, MAE = 2.7120); macaque |Delta brain age| negatively correlated with actual age (R = -0.3759, P < 0.001, MAE = 4.8697).

Interpretation: errors of the macaque model on humans grow with age, indicating evolutionary divergence accrues during human development; conversely, the human model fits older macaques better, suggesting mature macaque brains resemble young humans along the chronological axis.

subsections.title: Brain areas and white-matter tracts contributing to prediction
content_bullets: Context: prediction features can be partitioned into human-specific, macaque-specific, and shared features to locate cross-species divergence.
Finding (macaque-specific, GM): left PFCdl R = 0.4003, left PMCm R = 0.3966, right PFCdl R = 0.3723, right PFCom R = 0.3478, right PFCcl R = 0.3427; (WM) left SLF II R = 0.3067, right UF R = 0.2901.

Finding (human-specific, GM): left Putamen R = 0.4437, right Pallidum R = 0.4284, left posterior insula R = 0.3915, left Pallidum R = 0.3884, right S1 R = 0.3703; (WM) right SLF I R = 0.4138, left IFOF R = 0.3665, left AF R = 0.3459, right IFOF R = 0.3445, left SLF I R = 0.3443.

Finding (shared GM, human): right posterior insula R = 0.3769, left CCp R = 0.3583, left PMCvl R = 0.3523, right CCp R = 0.3466, right PCip R = 0.3433; (shared GM, macaque): right CCa R = 0.4694, left CCa R = 0.4274, right PMCm R = 0.4235, left PCs R = 0.4228, right PCi R = 0.4035.

Finding (shared WM, human): left CT R = 0.4654, right CT R = 0.4499, right STR R = 0.3344, left STR R = 0.3212, left Cs:Pg R = 0.2341; (shared WM, macaque): right CT R = 0.4189, left CT R = 0.3746, right UF R = 0.3497, left SLF II R = 0.3067, left STR R = 0.3046.

Interpretation: macaque-specific features concentrate in prefrontal/motor gray matter while human-specific features concentrate in association white-matter tracts, consistent with disproportionate white-matter development in humans.

subsections.title: BCAP, behavior, and evolution-associated structure
content_bullets: Context: to quantify individual cross-species developmental divergence, we defined BCAP as a percentile-rank difference between P-age_human-human and P-age_monkey-human.
Finding (behavior): BCAP correlated with picture vocabulary test (R = 0.1588, P = 0.0323) and visual sensitivity test (R = -0.2051, P = 0.0056).
Finding (WM, FA): positive: left AF R = 0.3784, left OR R = 0.3232, right AF R = 0.3035; negative: AC R = 0.1215, right SLF III R = 0.1166, FM R = -0.1221.

Finding (GM): positive: right PFCdl R = 0.4685, right PFCcl R = 0.4324, right PFCvl R = 0.4287, right PFCdm R = 0.3915, right PFCm R = 0.3890; negative: right Amyg R = -0.1759, right Acc R = -0.181, left Acc R = -0.1811, left Pu R = -0.2104, left Amyg R = -0.2151.

Interpretation: BCAP localizes evolution-associated structure to language pathways (AF) and higher-order prefrontal cortex, with negative weights in subcortical affective/limbic structures more predominant in macaques.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Summarize: a shared chronological age-prediction model reveals a stable cross-species imbalance, with macaque models predicting human age better than the converse.
Paragraph 1: the age-gap asymmetry implies early-developing brain features are more conserved than late-developing higher cognitive features; prediction errors accumulate during human adolescence.
Paragraph 2: BCAP as a percentile-rank index overcomes limitations of prior cortical expansion measures that gave inconsistent results across studies.

Paragraph 3: human-specific white-matter features (AF, IFOF, SLF I) support the language-evolution hypothesis; macaque-specific prefrontal/motor gray-matter features are consistent with visually guided motor control.

Paragraph 4: evolution-associated structure uncovered by BCAP (right prefrontal cortex, AF, OR) converges with prior findings on language and executive-control evolution; negative correlations in putamen/amygdala mark macaque-predominant circuitry.
Paragraph 5 (limitations/outlook): cross-species atlas choices (RM + XTRACT), narrow age windows (human 8-14 y; macaque 2-4 y), and need to extend BCAP to disease and broader lifespan cohorts.

subsections.title: Human and macaque subjects and MRI acquisition
content_bullets: 370 HCP-D humans (170 M/200 F; 8-14 y; 11.1 +/- 1.8 y), Siemens 3T Tim Trio, T1 TR/TE = 2500/2.22 ms, flip 8 deg, 0.8x0.8x0.8 mm; dMRI 185 dirs at b = 1500/3000 s/mm^2 plus 28 b0, TR/TE = 3230/89.2 ms, flip 78 deg, 1.5^3 mm, 92 slices.

181 macaques (89 M/92 F; 2-4 y; 2.8 +/- 0.6 y) at UW-Madison; 42 on GE DISCOVERY_MR750 3.0T, 139 on GE Signa EXCITE 3.0T. DISCOVERY dMRI: 12 dirs b ~ 1000, TE/TR 94.3/6100 ms, flip 90 deg, 0.5469x2.5x0.5469 mm; T1 TR/TE 11.4/5.412 ms, flip 10 deg, 0.2734x0.5x0.2734 mm. EXCITE dMRI: 12 dirs b ~ 1000, TE/TR 77.2/10000 ms, flip 90 deg, 0.5469x2.5x0.5469 mm; T1 TR/TE 8.648/1.888 ms, flip 10 deg, 0.2734x0.5x0.2734 mm.

subsections.title: Preprocessing, harmonization and feature extraction
content_bullets: GMV computed with SPM8 VBM; macaque skull-stripped and registered to INIA19 template; human normalized to MNI152 via DARTEL.
dMRI preprocessed in FSL (eddy_correct); FA/MD/AD/RD computed per voxel; registered to MNI152 (human) or INIA19 (macaque).
ComBat harmonization of macaque datasets to mitigate scanner differences.
92 gray-matter regions (RM atlas) + 42 white-matter tracts x 4 indices = 168 WM + 92 GM = 260 features per subject (XTRACT cross-species atlas).

subsections.title: Cross-species age prediction and BCAP
content_bullets: Feature selection: Pearson's correlation p < 0.01 between each feature and age; final features from (i) common across 100 iterations (62 macaque, 225 human) or (ii) minimum-MAE iteration (117 macaque, 239 human) or (iii) top 62 shared.
10-fold cross-validation, 100 repetitions; metrics Pearson R and MAE.
BCAP = percentile(P-age_human-human) - percentile(P-age_monkey-human).
Correlations of BCAP with 40 behavioral scales and per-region GMV/FA/MD/AD/RD, controlling for age, with FDR P < 0.05.
